repository_metadata.name: Covid-19-Vaccine-Time-Analysis-
repository_metadata.description: Predicted the time by which the entire population of India will be vaccinated, primarily based on Data Analysis using Python.

raw_text_evidence.readme_excerpt:
# COVID-19 Vaccine Time Analysis

This project analyzes COVID-19 vaccination progress in India and predicts the approximate time required to vaccinate the entire population. The analysis is performed using Python in a Jupyter Notebook, with a focus on time-series analysis, data preprocessing, visualization, and vaccination trend estimation.

## Project Overview

During the COVID-19 pandemic, vaccination progress became an important public health indicator. This project uses available vaccination data to study how vaccination numbers changed over time and estimate when India could reach full vaccination coverage based on observed trends.

## Objective

The main objectives of this project are:

- Analyze COVID-19 vaccination trends in India
- Understand the growth pattern of vaccine administration over time
- Perform time-series based prediction
- Estimate the time required to vaccinate the entire Indian population
- Visualize vaccination progress using Python libraries

## Repository Contents

```text
Covid-19-Vaccine-Time-Analysis-
│
└── IndiaCovidVaccine_TimeSeriesPrediction.ipynb
raw_text_evidence.description: Predicted the time by which the entire population of India will be vaccinated, primarily based on Data Analysis using Python.

repository_metadata.name: Sign-Language-Recognition-using-Deep-Learning
repository_metadata.description: A prototype capable of predicting and translating live sign language gestures into English using the LSTM neural networks, MediaPipe Holistic, OpenCV & Python.

raw_text_evidence.readme_excerpt:
# Sign Language Recognition using Deep Learning and Computer Vision

Real-time vision-based Sign Language Recognition system designed to assist individuals with hearing and speech impairments by translating hand gestures into readable text. This project aims to promote accessibility and inclusivity by bridging communication gaps.

# Project Overview

The system recognizes six basic sign gestures:

1. Hello

2. How are you

3. I’m sorry

4. Thank you

5. I love you

6. Goodbye

We used deep learning techniques with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and MediaPipe Holistic keypoint detection to achieve accurate gesture recognition in real-time.

# Problem Statement

Individuals with hearing or speech impairments often rely on sign language, which is not universally understood. This project addresses the need for a reliable, real-time sign language recognition system that can interpret gestures and output readable text without requiring human interpreters.

# Tools & Technologies

- Python

- OpenCV (cv2)

- MediaPipe Holistic

- TensorFlow & Keras

- NumPy, Sci-kit Learn, Matplotlib

# Methodology

1. Keypoint Detection with MediaPipe Holistic

- Extracted body, face, and hand landmarks (540+ points)

2. Dataset Creation

- Captured 30 videos per gesture (30 frames each)

- Converted sequences into TensorFlow-compatible format

3. Data Preprocessing & Labeling

- Split into training and testing sets

- One-hot encoding of gesture labels

4. Model Architecture

- 3 LSTM layers

- 2 Dense layers with ReLU activation

- Final Dense layer with Softmax activation

- Trained using Adam optimizer & categorical crossentropy

5. Model Evaluation

- Achieved categorical accuracy of ~79% during training

- Evaluated with Confusion Matrix and Accuracy Score

6. Real-time Testing

- Integrated OpenCV and MediaPipe for live gesture detection and text output

# Contributors

- Krittika Sardar

- Anjali Sinha

- Bobby Baidya

Supervised by Dr. Soham Sarkar
raw_text_evidence.description: A prototype capable of predicting and translating live sign language gestures into English using the LSTM neural networks, MediaPipe Holistic, OpenCV & Python.

repository_metadata.name: Handwritten-Digit-Recognition
repository_metadata.description: Deep learning project that recognizes handwritten digits from image data using Python, TensorFlow/Keras, and neural networks.

raw_text_evidence.readme_excerpt:
# Handwritten Digit Recognition

This project implements a deep learning model to recognize handwritten digits from image data. The model is trained on digit images and learns to classify each image into one of the digits from **0 to 9**.

## Project Overview

Handwritten digit recognition is a classic computer vision problem where the goal is to teach a machine to identify handwritten numbers. This project demonstrates how image data can be preprocessed, trained using a neural network, and evaluated for digit classification.

The project is useful for understanding the basics of:

- Image classification
- Deep learning
- Neural networks
- Computer vision
- Model training and evaluation

## Objective

The main objectives of this project are:

- Load and preprocess handwritten digit image data
- Build a neural network model for digit classification
- Train the model to recognize digits from 0 to 9
- Evaluate the model performance using accuracy and loss
- Test the model on unseen digit images

## Dataset

This project is based on handwritten digit image data, commonly represented using the MNIST dataset format. MNIST contains grayscale images of handwritten digits from 0 to 9 and is widely used for training and testing machine learning models. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

## Technologies Used

- Python
- Jupyter Notebook
- NumPy
- Pandas
- Matplotlib
- TensorFlow / Keras
- Deep Learning
- Artificial Neural Networks

## Key Features

- Image preprocessing and normalization
- Neural network model creation
- Model training and validation
- Accuracy and loss visualization
- Handwritten digit prediction
- Beginner-friendly deep learning implementation

## Repository Structure

```text
Handwritten-Digit-Recognition
│
├── handwritten_digit_recognition.ipynb
└── README.md

raw_text_evidence.readme_excerpt:
# Skill Mining – GitHub Profile Data Collector & Preprocessor

A Python pipeline that collects public GitHub profile data, cleans and chunks
it into RAG-ready evidence, and produces structured JSON output for downstream
skill/role/leadership inference by LLM agents.

---

## Project structure

```
skill_mining/
├── config.py                        # All configuration (reads from .env)
├── github_client.py                 # Authenticated client, retry, rate-limit helpers
├── main.py                          # CLI entry point (typer)
├── collectors/
│   ├── profile_collector.py         # User profile metadata
│   └── repo_collector.py            # Repositories, commits, READMEs, scoring
├── data/
│   ├── agents/                      # AI agent pipeline for profile analysis
│   │   ├── agents.py                # LangChain agents (skill, role, summarizer)
│   │   ├── pipeline.py              # LangGraph orchestration
│   │   └── tools.py                 # GitHub API utilities
│   ├── raw/                         # <username>.json (full evidence document)
│   ├── processed/                   # <username>_summary.json, master_summary.csv
│   └── preprocessed/                # <username>_preprocessed.json (RAG-ready chunks)
├── transformers_local/
│   └── schema_builder.py            # Assembles final JSON schema
├── preprocessor/
│   ├── cleaner.py                   # Fix encoding, strip noise, drop empty items
│   ├── chunker.py                   # Split long evidence into ≤1500-char chunks
│   ├── historical.py                # Temporal analysis (commits/languages by year)
│   └── pipeline.py                  # Orchestrates clean → chunk → historical analysis
├── utils/
│   └── helpers.py                   # Shared utility functions
├── logs/                            # run_<timestamp>.json
├── usernames.txt                    # 10 individual GitHub profiles for experiments
├── requirements.txt
├── .env.example
└── README.md
```

---

## Setup

### 1. Clone / create the project

```bash
cd skill_mining
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate      # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 2. Configure your API keys

```bash
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env and set:
# GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_github_token_here
# GROQ_API_KEY=your_groq_api_key_here

Never commit your `.env` file. Copy `.env.example` to `.env` and fill in your own API keys. The `.env` file is listed in `.gitignore` and will not be pushed to GitHub.
```

#### GitHub Token
Generate a **classic personal access token** with **public repo read** scope at: https://github.com/settings/tokens

#### Groq API Key
Get your API key for AI agent analysis at: https://console.groq.com/keys

### 3. Verify setup

```bash
python main.py rate-limit
```

---

## Usage

### Collect a single profile

```bash
python main.py collect --username torvalds
```

### Collect multiple profiles from a file

```bash
python main.py collect --file usernames.txt
```

### Also generate Markdown summaries for manual inspection

```bash
python main.py collect --file usernames.txt --markdown
```

### Enable disk caching (avoids re-fetching during development)

```bash
ENABLE_CACHE=true python main.py collect --file usernames.txt
```

---

## Preprocessing

After collection, run the preprocessor to clean, chunk, and analyse the raw data.
This produces the `data/preprocessed/` files consumed by the retrieval (ChromaDB) step.

### Preprocess a single user

```bash
python main.py preprocess --username karpathy
```

### Preprocess all collected users at once

```bash
python main.py preprocess --all
```

### Adjust chunk size (default 1500 characters)

```bash
python main.py preprocess --all --chunk-size 1000
```

The preprocessor runs three steps internally:

| Step | Module | What it does |
|------|--------|--------------|
| Clean | `preprocessor/cleaner.py` | Fixes encoding corruption, strips HTML and badge lines, drops items < 20 chars |
| Chunk | `preprocessor/chunker.py … [truncated]

repository_metadata.name: structured-information-benchmark
repository_metadata.description: Benchmark pipeline for evaluating how well LLMs handle structured information tasks compared with SQL-based ground truth using IMDb data.

raw_text_evidence.readme_excerpt:
# Benchmark for Structured Information Processing in Large Language Models

> University of Mannheim - Team Project FSS 2026  
> School of Business Informatics and Mathematics  
> Supervisor: Dr. Sven Hertling

## Project Overview

This project builds a **benchmark pipeline** to evaluate how well Large Language Models (LLMs) handle structured information tasks — compared to using SQL queries on a real relational database.

The core hypothesis: tasks that are **hard for LLMs alone** (aggregations, multi-hop queries, large data) become **easy and reliable** when an LLM is combined with a structured database (Text2SQL approach).

## Pipeline Flow

```
Raw IMDb Data
     │
     ▼
Cleaned IMDb Subset  ──────────────────────────────────────┐
     │                                                       │
     ▼                                                       │
Synthetic Data                                   Unstructured Wikipedia Data
     │                                                       │
     ▼                                                       │
SQLite Database ◄──────────────────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ├──► Benchmark Tasks (natural language questions)
     │         │
     │         ├──► SQL Ground Truth Answers
     │         │
     │         └──► LLM Prompts  ──► LLM Output
     │
     ▼
Evaluation Table (SQL answer vs LLM answer)
```

## Folder Structure

```
structured-information-benchmark/
│
├── data/
│   ├── raw/                          # Original datasets (large raw files not pushed)
│   ├── processed/                    # Cleaned IMDb subset files
│   ├── synthetic/                    # Synthetic benchmark data from preparation/team
│   ├── unstructured/                 # Wikipedia text, HTML, screenshots, other formats
│   │   ├── wikipedia_text/
│   │   └── wikipedia_html/
│   └── benchmark_tasks/              # Task definitions with SQL links
│
├── database/                         # Local SQLite files (not pushed)
├── sql/
│   └── ground_truth_queries/         # SQL files used for factual answer generation
├── prompts/
│   └── task_prompts/                 # Reusable prompts for experiments
├── results/
│   ├── sql_ground_truth/             # SQL output answer keys
│   ├── llm_outputs/
│   │   └── gpt_5_3/                  # Model-specific LLM responses
│   └── evaluation/                   # Comparison tables and metrics
├── notebooks/                        # Exploratory notebooks
├── src/                              # Python pipeline scripts
│   ├── data_preparation.py
│   ├── synthetic_data_preparation.py
│   ├── create_database.py
│   ├── generate_sql_groundtruth.py
│   ├── run_llm_experiments.py
│   └── evaluate_results.py
├── docs/                             # Team docs and workflow notes
├── config.yaml                       # Central configuration file
├── main.py                           # Pipeline entry point
├── requirements.txt                  # Python dependencies
├── .env.example                      # API key placeholders (never real keys)
└── .gitignore
```

## Quickstart

### 1. Clone the repository
```bash
git clone https://github.com/<your-org>/structured-information-benchmark.git
cd structured-information-benchmark
```

### 2. Create a virtual environment
```bash
python -m venv venv

# Windows (Git Bash)
source venv/Scripts/activate

# macOS / Linux
source venv/bin/activate
```

### 3. Install dependencies
```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 4. Set up environment variables
```bash
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env and fill in your API keys
```

### 5. Download IMDb data
Visit https://datasets.imdbws.com/ and download:
- `title.basics.tsv.gz`
- `title.ratings.tsv.gz`
- `name.basics.tsv.gz`
- `title.crew.tsv.gz`
- `title.principals.tsv.gz`

Place the extracted `.tsv` files in `data/raw/`.

### 6. Run the full pipeline
```bash
python main.py
```

Or run individual steps:
```bash
python src/data_preparation.py
python src/create_database.py
python src/ge … [truncated]

repository_metadata.name: Sentiment-Analysis-on-Amazon-Fine-Food-Reviews
repository_metadata.description: Sentiment analysis on the Amazon Fine Food Reviews dataset using traditional ML, LSTM, and transformer-based models. This project compares TF-IDF and Word2Vec vectorizations with various class balancing techniques to evaluate model performance on multi-class sentiment classification tasks.

raw_text_evidence.readme_excerpt:
# Sentiment-Analysis-on-Amazon-Fine-Food-Reviews

The uploaded code includes model implementations and feature engineering workflows based on both TF-IDF and Word2Vec representations.

# Project Overview

This project performs sentiment analysis on the Amazon Fine Food Reviews dataset using a combination of traditional machine learning, deep learning, and transformer-based models. The objective is to classify reviews as Positive, Neutral, or Negative, leveraging various NLP techniques and web mining methods to analyze large-scale consumer feedback.

# Dataset

- Source: [Kaggle - Amazon Fine Food Reviews](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/snap/amazon-fine-food-reviews/data)

- Size: ~568,000 reviews spanning over 10 years

- Features used: Review Text, Summary, and Score

# Sentiment Mapping:

- Positive (Score > 3)

- Neutral (Score = 3)

- Negative (Score < 3)

# Preprocessing Steps

- Removed null values and duplicates

- Cleaned HTML tags and punctuations

- Stopwords filtered with negations preserved

- Handled class imbalance using sampling techniques

- Data split into 80:20 train-test sets

# Feature Engineering

- TF-IDF

- Word2Vec embeddings

# Models Implemented

- Logistic Regression (baseline)

- Naive Bayes

- Random Forest

- Support Vector Machines (SVM)

- LSTM (deep learning)

- Transformer Models: BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT

# Key Findings

DistilBERT delivered the best overall performance, especially on positive and neutral sentiments.

TF-IDF worked best with traditional models, while LSTM and transformers benefitted from dense embeddings.

Class balancing significantly impacted model performance.

# Future Scope

- Explore ensemble models

- Advanced fine-tuning of transformer models

- Experiment with sentiment lexicons

# Contributors

- Sushma Kulkarni

- Krittika Sardar

- Mansi Sawant

- Nishit Suthar

- Eljes Basha

## Project Overview

- Analyze COVID-19 vaccination trends in India

# Sign Language Recognition using Deep Learning and Computer Vision

Real-time vision-based Sign Language Recognition system designed to assist individuals with hearing and speech impairments by translating hand gestures into readable text. This project aims to promote accessibility and inclusivity by bridging communication gaps.

# Project Overview

The system recognizes six basic sign gestures:

1. Hello

2. How are you

3. I’m sorry

4. Thank you

5. I love you

6. Goodbye

We used deep learning techniques with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and MediaPipe Holistic keypoint detection to achieve accurate gesture recognition in real-time.

# Problem Statement

Individuals with hearing or speech impairments often rely on sign language, which is not universally understood. This project addresses the need for a reliable, real-time sign language recognition system that can interpret gestures and output readable text without requiring human interpreters.

# Tools & Technologies

- Python

- OpenCV (cv2)

- MediaPipe Holistic

- TensorFlow & Keras

- NumPy, Sci-kit Learn, Matplotlib

# Methodology

1. Keypoint Detection with MediaPipe Holistic

- Extracted body, face, and hand landmarks (540+ points)

2. Dataset Creation

- Captured 30 videos per gesture (30 frames each)

- Converted sequences into TensorFlow-compatible format

3. Data Preprocessing & Labeling

- Split into training and testing sets

- One-hot encoding of gesture labels

4. Model Architecture

- 3 LSTM layers

- 2 Dense layers with ReLU activation

- Final Dense layer with Softmax activation

- Trained using Adam optimizer & categorical crossentropy

5. Model Evaluation

Deep learning project that recognizes handwritten digits from image data using Python, TensorFlow/Keras, and neural networks.

# Handwritten Digit Recognition

This project implements a deep learning model to recognize handwritten digits from image data. The model is trained on digit images and learns to classify each image into one of the digits from **0 to 9**.

## Project Overview

Handwritten digit recognition is a classic computer vision problem where the goal is to teach a machine to identify handwritten numbers. This project demonstrates how image data can be preprocessed, trained using a neural network, and evaluated for digit classification.

The project is useful for understanding the basics of:

- Image classification
- Deep learning
- Neural networks
- Computer vision
- Model training and evaluation

## Objective

The main objectives of this project are:

- Load and preprocess handwritten digit image data
- Build a neural network model for digit classification
- Train the model to recognize digits from 0 to 9
- Evaluate the model performance using accuracy and loss
- Test the model on unseen digit images

## Dataset

This project is based on handwritten digit image data, commonly represented using the MNIST dataset format. MNIST contains grayscale images of handwritten digits from 0 to 9 and is widely used for training and testing machine learning models. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

## Technologies Used

- Python
- Jupyter Notebook
- NumPy
- Pandas
- Matplotlib
- TensorFlow / Keras
- Deep Learning
- Artificial Neural Networks

## Key Features

- Image preprocessing and normalization
- Neural network model creation
- Model training and validation
- Accuracy and loss visualization
- Handwritten digit prediction
- Beginner-friendly deep learning implementation

# Skill Mining – GitHub Profile Data Collector & Preprocessor

A Python pipeline that collects public GitHub profile data, cleans and chunks
it into RAG-ready evidence, and produces structured JSON output for downstream
skill/role/leadership inference by LLM agents.

---

## Project structure

```
skill_mining/
├── config.py                        # All configuration (reads from .env)
├── github_client.py                 # Authenticated client, retry, rate-limit helpers
├── main.py                          # CLI entry point (typer)
├── collectors/
│   ├── profile_collector.py         # User profile metadata
│   └── repo_collector.py            # Repositories, commits, READMEs, scoring
├── data/
│   ├── agents/                      # AI agent pipeline for profile analysis
│   │   ├── agents.py                # LangChain agents (skill, role, summarizer)
│   │   ├── pipeline.py              # LangGraph orchestration
│   │   └── tools.py                 # GitHub API utilities

│   ├── processed/                   # <username>_summary.json, master_summary.csv
│   └── preprocessed/                # <username>_preprocessed.json (RAG-ready chunks)

## Setup

Generate a **classic personal access token** with **public repo read** scope at: https://github.com/settings/tokens

#### Groq API Key
Get your API key for AI agent analysis at: https://console.groq.com/keys

### 3. Verify setup

```bash
python main.py rate-limit
```

---

## Usage

### Collect a single profile

```bash
python main.py collect --username torvalds
```

### Collect multiple profiles from a file

```bash
python main.py collect --file usernames.txt
```

### Also generate Markdown summaries for manual inspection

```bash
python main.py collect --file usernames.txt --markdown
```

### Enable disk caching (avoids re-fetching during development)

```bash
ENABLE_CACHE=true python main.py collect --file usernames.txt
```

---

## Preprocessing

After collection, run the preprocessor to clean, chunk, and analyse the raw data.
This produces the `data/preprocessed/` files consumed by the retrieval (ChromaDB) step.

### Preprocess a single user

```bash
python main.py preprocess --username karpathy
```

### Preprocess all collected users at once

```bash
python main.py preprocess --all
```

### Adjust chunk size (default 1500 characters)

```bash
python main.py preprocess --all --chunk-size 1000
```

The preprocessor runs three steps internally:

| Step | Module | What it does |
|------|--------|--------------|
| Clean | `preprocessor/cleaner.py` | Fixes encoding corruption, strips HTML and badge lines, drops items < 20 chars |
| Chunk | `preprocessor/chunker.py … [truncated]

Benchmark pipeline for evaluating how well LLMs handle structured information tasks compared with SQL-based ground truth using IMDb data.

# Benchmark for Structured Information Processing in Large Language Models

> University of Mannheim - Team Project FSS 2026  
> School of Business Informatics and Mathematics  
> Supervisor: Dr. Sven Hertling

## Project Overview

This project builds a **benchmark pipeline** to evaluate how well Large Language Models (LLMs) handle structured information tasks — compared to using SQL queries on a real relational database.

The core hypothesis: tasks that are **hard for LLMs alone** (aggregations, multi-hop queries, large data) become **easy and reliable** when an LLM is combined with a structured database (Text2SQL approach).

## Pipeline Flow

```
Raw IMDb Data
     │
     ▼
Cleaned IMDb Subset  ──────────────────────────────────────┐
     │                                                       │
     ▼                                                       │
Synthetic Data                                   Unstructured Wikipedia Data
     │                                                       │
     ▼                                                       │
SQLite Database ◄──────────────────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ├──► Benchmark Tasks (natural language questions)
     │         │
     │         ├──► SQL Ground Truth Answers
     │         │
     │         └──► LLM Prompts  ──► LLM Output
     │
     ▼
Evaluation Table (SQL answer vs LLM answer)
```

## Folder Structure

```
structured-information-benchmark/
│
├── data/
│   ├── raw/                          # Original datasets (large raw files not pushed)
│   ├── processed/                    # Cleaned IMDb subset files
│   ├── synthetic/                    # Synthetic benchmark data from preparation/team
│   ├── unstructured/                 # Wikipedia text, HTML, screenshots, other formats
│   │   ├── wikipedia_text/
│   │   └── wikipedia_html/
│   └── benchmark_tasks/              # Task definitions with SQL links
│
├── database/                         # Local SQLite files (not pushed)
├── sql/
│   └── ground_truth_queries/         # SQL files used for factual answer generation
├── prompts/
│   └── task_prompts/                 # Reusable prompts for experiments
├── results/
│   ├── sql_ground_truth/             # SQL output answer keys
│   ├── llm_outputs/
│   │   └── gpt_5_3/                  # Model-specific LLM responses
│   └── evaluation/                   # Comparison tables and metrics
├── notebooks/                        # Exploratory notebooks
├── src/                              # Python pipeline scripts
│   ├── data_preparation.py
│   ├── synthetic_data_preparation.py
│   ├── create_database.py
│   ├── generate_sql_groundtruth.py
│   ├── run_llm_experiments.py
│   └── evaluate_results.py
├── docs/                             # Team docs and workflow notes
├── config.yaml                       # Central configuration file
├── main.py                           # Pipeline entry point
├── requirements.txt                  # Python dependencies
├── .env.example                      # API key placeholders (never real keys)
└── .gitignore
```

## Quickstart

### 1. Clone the repository
```bash
git clone https://github.com/<your-org>/structured-information-benchmark.git
cd structured-information-benchmark
```

### 2. Create a virtual environment
```bash
python -m venv venv

# Windows (Git Bash)
source venv/Scripts/activate

# macOS / Linux
source venv/bin/activate
```

### 3. Install dependencies
```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 4. Set up environment variables
```bash
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env and fill in your API keys
```

### 5. Download IMDb data
Visit https://datasets.imdbws.com/ and download:
- `title.basics.tsv.gz`
- `title.ratings.tsv.gz`
- `name.basics.tsv.gz`
- `title.crew.tsv.gz`
- `title.principals.tsv.gz`

Place the extracted `.tsv` files in `data/raw/`.

### 6. Run the full pipeline
```bash
python main.py
```

Or run individual steps:
```bash
python src/data_preparation.py
python src/create_database.py
python src/ge … [truncated]

Sentiment analysis on the Amazon Fine Food Reviews dataset using traditional ML, LSTM, and transformer-based models. This project compares TF-IDF and Word2Vec vectorizations with various class balancing techniques to evaluate model performance on multi-class sentiment classification tasks.

# Sentiment-Analysis-on-Amazon-Fine-Food-Reviews

The uploaded code includes model implementations and feature engineering workflows based on both TF-IDF and Word2Vec representations.

# Project Overview

This project performs sentiment analysis on the Amazon Fine Food Reviews dataset using a combination of traditional machine learning, deep learning, and transformer-based models. The objective is to classify reviews as Positive, Neutral, or Negative, leveraging various NLP techniques and web mining methods to analyze large-scale consumer feedback.

# Dataset

- Source: [Kaggle - Amazon Fine Food Reviews](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/snap/amazon-fine-food-reviews/data)

- Size: ~568,000 reviews spanning over 10 years

- Features used: Review Text, Summary, and Score

# Sentiment Mapping:

- Positive (Score > 3)

- Neutral (Score = 3)

- Negative (Score < 3)

# Preprocessing Steps

- Removed null values and duplicates

- Cleaned HTML tags and punctuations

- Stopwords filtered with negations preserved

- Handled class imbalance using sampling techniques

- Data split into 80:20 train-test sets

# Feature Engineering

- TF-IDF

- Word2Vec embeddings

# Models Implemented

- Logistic Regression (baseline)

- Naive Bayes

- Random Forest

- Support Vector Machines (SVM)

- LSTM (deep learning)

- Transformer Models: BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT

# Key Findings

DistilBERT delivered the best overall performance, especially on positive and neutral sentiments.

TF-IDF worked best with traditional models, while LSTM and transformers benefitted from dense embeddings.

Class balancing significantly impacted model performance.

# Future Scope

- Explore ensemble models

- Advanced fine-tuning of transformer models

- Experiment with sentiment lexicons

# Contributors

- Sushma Kulkarni

- Krittika Sardar

- Mansi Sawant

- Nishit Suthar

- Eljes Basha
